W1.target: Ding ding ding! Lithuania is correct! 🇱🇹 The dish is called cepelinai (sep-elle-inn-eye) and it's basically a large grated potato dumpling filled with meat. 😋
W1.action_text: Yay! I’m woefully undereducated about Lithuania, but (not a speaker) I’m familiar with Lithuanian as a language because of its grammar and unique position as an Indo-European language with incredibly conservative features. The tone-accent contouring alone is legendary.

W1.target: Hun, the fact that you already know that much puts you well ahead over like 98% of non-Lithuanians already 😄 Because none of that is wrong in the least! I should know because I'm functionally fluent (although not completely due to growing up in Canada... but mostly).
W1.action_text: Language is my hobby (and my degree), so I have a lot of reference grammars. This is a reprint of an old text (mid 60’s) that I have. It’s surprising when some languages don’t have a lot of more modern materials.

W1.target: Oh wow, how interesting is that 😮 I didn't even know that book existed. But I'm not shocked there aren't more modern materials considering it's a language from a small country that really only nationals and their families speak.
W1.action_text: I shouldn’t have looked… 😔 Routledge, a British publisher that has a range of comprehensive grammars, published a 350+ page grammar of Lithuanian in 2020. It’s US$90.

"Two of Maxine G." My dad took this photo around 1958-59 of an angry Maxine (my mother). He was such an ass that it has to be his fault. Scanned from a color faded Ektachrome slide. The other photo is of her reincarnation as my cat. #photography #PhotographyArt #gayartists #Queerart Art

"The Winter of Discontent" I did this collage in November 2005 for my first blog. I was not consciously aware of impending personal disaster, which lay only days away.😭😭😭 I was never the same afterwards. My subconscious was ringing 'five alarm disaster rapidly approaching'. #Queerart Art

W1.target: The kind of people who say this will also share awful Facebook poems about how they grew up with no TV or heating and how life was better then. The problem with being nostalgic for childhood is that you’re basically yearning for a time when you weren’t fully part of reality. It’s all fantasy.
W1.action_text: I wouldn’t say children aren’t part of reality, but I would say nostalgia serves a very specific purpose, sometimes almost protecting us from the truth of what we experienced. Easier to say it was all fine that discuss numbers of child deaths/long term illness due to poor housing, for example.

W2.action_text: Again, though, I think children are often very aware, but as adults they shut themselves off from some of their experiences as a form of self preservation, easier than accepting things could have ended very differently. Evening walk.
Everything's frost-twinkly already, the damp lane becoming treacherous-slick.

W2.target: 📢📢📢📢📢 JEWS!! Reminder!! If fasting for Yom Kippur would harm you in ANY way—physically, but also psychologically, emotionally, etc— it is quite literally a mitzvah to eat. Amazing resources here:
I fast from food on Yom Kippur but I still drink water because I have POTS. I also keep salty snacks on hand Just In Case I have a flare. What’s the saying? Live by the mitzvot, don’t die by them.

My synagogue has a little snack and water station during Yom Kippur for people who can’t fast or need to break their fast for health reasons. It’s wonderful to be in a Jewish community where I know I won’t be looked at weird if I need to modify my fast.
W2.action_text: wow, that's actually really amazing

This is especially important for people who are diabetic. If you're diabetic, fasting can be extremely dangerous because you can end up in a dangerous spot event though you physically "feel fine". Talk to your doctor, obviously, but don't jeopardize your health/life if that's a risk.
